questions.question: Why is a URL shortener usually much more constrained by read-path design than by alias generation logic?
options: Because redirect traffic is often massively higher than create traffic, especially during viral spikes.
Because alias generators require cross-region consensus for every request.
Because most shorteners never need a persistent metadata store.
Because custom aliases eliminate caching from the design.
questions.explanation: The hard operational problem is serving redirects cheaply and safely under skew.

questions.question: What is the main reason to add idempotency keys to link-creation requests?
options: They reduce redirect latency by avoiding cache lookups.
They keep retries from creating multiple short links for the same logical create operation.
They make analytics perfectly real time.
They remove the need for uniqueness checks on aliases.
questions.explanation: Create retries happen in the real world, and duplicate link creation is a correctness bug.

questions.question: Which change most directly protects the system when one short code becomes globally viral?
options: Move analytics writes into the synchronous redirect path.
Replicate hot entries through edge or cache layers instead of relying on one backend lookup path.
Shorten alias length so clients type less.
Disable custom aliases for all users.
questions.explanation: Viral-link protection is mainly a hot-key mitigation and caching problem.

questions.question: Why is asynchronous click logging often the right default for redirect services?
options: It lowers redirect latency and isolates analytics backpressure from end-user navigation.
It guarantees every click event is stored before the redirect response is sent.
It removes the need for abuse monitoring entirely.
It ensures cache entries never go stale.
questions.explanation: The redirect path should stay thin unless the business case forces synchronous accounting.

questions.question: Which metric best exposes hot-link concentration before it becomes an outage?
options: The share of total redirect traffic handled by the top N short codes.
Average link title length across the database.
The number of employees who deployed this week.
The median create payload byte size.
questions.explanation: Skew metrics reveal when one code dominates capacity.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with long cache TTLs on redirect metadata?
options: They reduce backend load but make fast revocation or destination edits harder to propagate.
They increase alias entropy but lower durability.
They guarantee malware verdicts are always current.
They make write-path uniqueness checks unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Read efficiency improves, but freshness-sensitive changes become harder.

questions.question: Why should abuse screening happen before a newly created link is activated?
options: So the service can block unsafe destinations before the redirect path starts serving them.
So click analytics can be skipped for all paid users.
So the database never needs secondary indexes.
So custom aliases become impossible to guess.
questions.explanation: Activation-time screening limits the blast radius of malicious links.

questions.question: Which design decision is most likely to hurt redirect latency with little product value?
options: Resolving the code from cache and emitting analytics asynchronously.
Requiring the redirect path to wait for a downstream analytics warehouse write.
Using a metadata store for durable link ownership.
Keeping revocation state in the same control plane as link metadata.
questions.explanation: Warehouses are the wrong dependency for a user-facing redirect path.
